ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for teriyaki salmon beef rice bowl; closest defensible match is the generic rice bowl dish class FOODON_00002809.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category for a mixed salmon-beef teriyaki rice bowl; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this specific dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Japanese rice bowl dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of approximately 500 g, comprising steamed white rice topped with grilled teriyaki-glazed salmon and simmered beef, as typically served at Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: composite estimate for teriyaki salmon beef rice bowl derived from cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), Atlantic salmon grilled (FDC ID 175167), beef chuck simmered (FDC ID 174032), and teriyaki sauce (FDC ID 172237)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for a teriyaki salmon bowl with beef. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked white rice (~50% by weight, FDC ID 168878), grilled Atlantic salmon (~25%, FDC ID 175167), simmered beef chuck (~15%, FDC ID 174032), and teriyaki sauce (~10%, FDC ID 172237). Input per_100g values from GrabFood (confidence 0.7, provenance ai_generated) were cross-validated against this USDA composite and retained where consistent. Values for monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and potassium are estimated from the USDA component breakdown. GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central is the sole approved nutrient evidence base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (boiled, average, GI 64)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a teriyaki salmon beef rice bowl. GI estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~64, University of Sydney GI Database average for boiled white rice; Atkinson et al. 2008). Salmon, beef, and teriyaki sauce contribute negligible additional carbohydrate-driven GI impact. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI 64 × available carbohydrate per serving 72 g) / 100 = 46.1. Available carbohydrate per serving = 14.4 g/100g × 500 g / 100 = 72 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 267 mg/100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1,335 mg sodium, representing 58% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg, driven primarily by teriyaki sauce and soy sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced teriyaki and soy sauce application, choose lean beef cuts to minimise saturated fat-related arterial stiffness, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load of 46.1 per 500 g serving driven by steamed white rice (GI 64) with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g, approximately 3.5 g per serving), resulting in rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, request reduced teriyaki sauce to lower added sugar intake, and increase the proportion of salmon relative to rice to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), partially offsetting the saturated fat contribution from simmered beef (1.3 g saturated fat/100 g) and the high sodium load from teriyaki sauce.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may benefit from the omega-3 content of salmon in this dish but should request reduced sauce to lower sodium, choose lean beef cuts to minimise saturated fat, and limit portion size to manage overall energy and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef contains arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6), counteracting the anti-inflammatory omega-3 contribution from salmon.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider requesting a salmon-only version of this bowl without beef, substituting white rice with brown rice, and retaining sesame seed garnish for its anti-inflammatory lignan content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki Salmon Bowl with Beef (テリヤキサーモン牛丼; 照烧三文鱼牛肉饭; Mangkuk Nasi Salmon Teriyaki dengan Daging Lembu) is a Japanese-inspired rice bowl dish served at fast-casual Japanese restaurant chains such as Sukiya in Singapore. The dish consists of a bed of steamed white rice topped with two protein components: grilled Atlantic salmon fillets glazed with sweet-savoury teriyaki sauce (a mixture of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar), and Sukiya-style simmered beef (thinly sliced beef chuck braised in a dashi-soy-mirin broth with onions). The combination of grilled fish and simmered beef over rice creates a layered umami flavour profile with a sweet glaze, tender beef, and flaky salmon. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 8.9 g protein, 6.2 g fat (1.3 g saturated), 14.4 g carbohydrate, 3.3 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 267 mg sodium. A standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 720 kcal and 72 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of 46.1, making it a high-GL meal. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium due to teriyaki and soy sauces, making it unsuitable for hypertension without sauce reduction. The white rice base creates a high glycaemic load unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management without substitution. Salmon contributes cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), but the beef component adds arachidonic acid (omega-6), making the dish pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is not halal-certified due to mirin and sake content.

confidence_reason: Input data originated from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Nutrient values were cross-validated against a USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for the component ingredients (cooked white rice FDC 168878, grilled Atlantic salmon FDC 175167, simmered beef chuck FDC 174032, teriyaki sauce FDC 172237) and found to be broadly consistent. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. GI is estimated from the white rice dominant carbohydrate source using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods) weighted composite of cooked white rice (~50%, FDC ID 168878), grilled Atlantic salmon (~25%, FDC ID 175167), simmered beef chuck (~15%, FDC ID 174032), and teriyaki sauce (~10%, FDC ID 172237) by weight. GrabFood input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against this USDA composite and retained where consistent; GrabFood is not used as a primary nutrient source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from USDA component breakdown and not directly reported in the input; potassium_mg (220) is estimated from salmon, beef, and onion components per USDA SR Legacy. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish preparation is not available as a single confirmed entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component values exist but reliable weighted composite figures for this specific dish cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 4. The input contained two conflicting default portions (550 g and 450 g, both marked is_default: true); a single representative serving of 500 g was adopted as the standard serving size for GL calculation. 5. The dish contains mirin and sake (alcohol-containing ingredients) and is therefore not halal-certified. 6. Ethnic tags in the input ('Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') were removed as they are irrelevant to this dish. 7. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. 8. This dish is served in Singapore at Japanese fast-casual chains; multilingual local names provided for Japanese (mandatory), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy Singapore multilingual coverage requirements.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name. Japanese mandatory local-language name present (テリヤキサーモン牛丼). Singapore-context multilingual names provided: Chinese simplified (照烧三文鱼牛肉饭), Chinese traditional (照燒三文魚牛肉飯), Malay (Mangkuk Nasi Salmon Teriyaki dengan Daging Lembu), Tamil (தெரியாக்கி சால்மன் மாட்டிறைச்சி சாதக் கிண்ணம்), Hindi (तेरियाकी सैल्मन बीफ राइस बाउल). All mandatory language fields populated.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (144.0), protein_g (8.9), fat_g (6.2), carbohydrate_g (14.4) all present and numeric. nutrient_source documents USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods composite estimate with component FDC IDs cited. GrabFood explicitly excluded as primary source. Approved source compliance confirmed.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.7), sodium_mg (267.0), saturated_fat_g (1.3), sugar_g (3.3) all present as numeric values, plausible and supported by USDA composite estimate.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (64), gi_category (medium), glycemic_load_per_serving (46.1), gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via analogous estimate for white rice dominant carbohydrate (Atkinson et al. 2008); estimation basis fully documented. Approved GI source confirmed.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl dish, closest_parent match) and FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based composite dish, closest_parent match) provided with honest gap notes. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,500 characters), explains dish identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, GL-driven, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes', machine key type2_diabetes), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, omega-3 vs sodium/saturated fat balance). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
